---
title: "Week 1 ‚Äî Command Line Basics"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Open a terminal (Terminal on macOS/Linux, Git Bash on Windows)
- Navigate the file system using `cd`, `ls`/`dir`, and `pwd`
- Create directories with `mkdir`
- Create, move, and rename files
- Understand absolute vs relative paths

## Why the Command Line?

The command line is the universal interface to your computer. Every tool we use ‚Äî R, Python, Git, Quarto ‚Äî can be controlled via the command line. Learning it early means you can troubleshoot any tool later.

## Core Concepts

### The Terminal

The terminal is a text-based interface to your computer. You type commands; the computer responds.

### File System Navigation

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `pwd` | Print working directory (where am I?) | `pwd` |
| `ls` | List files in current directory | `ls` or `ls -la` |
| `cd` | Change directory | `cd Documents` |
| `cd ..` | Go up one directory | `cd ..` |
| `cd ~` | Go to home directory | `cd ~` |

### File Operations

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `mkdir` | Make directory | `mkdir my-project` |
| `touch` | Create empty file | `touch notes.txt` |
| `mv` | Move or rename | `mv old.txt new.txt` |
| `cp` | Copy | `cp file.txt backup.txt` |
| `rm` | Remove file | `rm file.txt` |
| `rm -r` | Remove directory | `rm -r my-folder` |

## Exercise

### Part 1: Navigation

1. Open your terminal
2. Type `pwd` ‚Äî note the full path
3. Type `ls` ‚Äî see what files/folders are here
4. Type `cd ~` ‚Äî go to home directory
5. Type `pwd` again ‚Äî confirm you're at home

### Part 2: File Operations

1. Create a directory: `mkdir mds-intro`
2. Enter it: `cd mds-intro`
3. Create a file: `touch notes.txt` (macOS/Linux) or `type nul > notes.txt` (Windows)
4. List it: `ls` ‚Äî should see `notes.txt`
5. Rename it: `mv notes.txt week1-notes.txt`
6. Create a subdirectory: `mkdir exercises`
7. Move the file into it: `mv week1-notes.txt exercises/`
8. Navigate in: `cd exercises`
9. List: `ls` ‚Äî should see your file

### Part 3: Relative vs Absolute Paths

From `~/mds-intro/exercises/`, try:

- `pwd` ‚Äî shows absolute path
- `cd ..` ‚Äî goes up to `mds-intro`
- `cd ../..` ‚Äî goes up two levels
- `cd ~/mds-intro/exercises` ‚Äî absolute path back

## Common Pitfalls

| Problem | Cause | Solution |
|---------|-------|----------|
| "Command not found" | Typo or not in PATH | Check spelling; ensure tool is installed |
| "No such file" | Wrong path | Use `pwd` to see where you are; use `ls` to see files |
| Spaces in names | Unescaped spaces | Quote paths: `cd "My Folder"` or escape: `cd My\ Folder` |

## Verification

Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

- [ ] Open terminal independently
- [ ] Navigate to any directory using `cd`
- [ ] Create a folder and a file
- [ ] Move and rename files
- [ ] Explain the difference between `cd ..` and `cd ~`

## Resources

- **macOS/Linux:** [Command Line Crash Course](https://learnpythonthehardway.org/book/appendix-a-cli/ex1.html)
- **Windows:** [Git Bash commands](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-bash)

---

[‚Üê Back to Intro Course](../index.qmd)
